logInvalidGitTreePath logs via slog.Warn, which bypasses the repo’s cli/logging package (and can end up on stderr rather than in .entire/logs/). Consider routing this through logging.Warn (e.g., with context.Background() or by threading a ctx into callers) to keep operational logs consistent with the rest of the codebase (see cmd/entire/cli/checkpoint/committed.go where warnings go through logging.Warn).

ReadCheckpointMetadata loads into memory a metadata json file and then unmarshal it. It goes on to then do the same for its sessions, which can be many and large files. Overall this approach has a huge impact on memory churn.

ReadCheckpointMetadata loads into memory a metadata json file and then unmarshal it. It goes on to then do the same for its sessions, which can be many and large files. Overall this approach has a huge impact on memory churn.

logInvalidGitTreePath logs via slog.Warn, which bypasses the repo’s cli/logging package (and can end up on stderr rather than in .entire/logs/). Consider routing this through logging.Warn (e.g., with context.Background() or by threading a ctx into callers) to keep operational logs consistent with the rest of the codebase (see cmd/entire/cli/checkpoint/committed.go where warnings go through logging.Warn).

I'd like you to go over the session logic especially the one that figures out if a session has ended and see if we can do improvements there. Basically we need a better / more reliable way a session ended. The main issue here is a user doing splitted commits after the session and the need to keep shadow branches around to continue to be able to do association. Maybe there is a better way

ReadCheckpointMetadata loads into memory a metadata json file and then unmarshal it. It goes on to then do the same for its sessions, which can be many and large files. Overall this approach has a huge impact on memory churn.
